content: Cancer screening aims to detect cancer before symptoms develop, when treatment is more effective. Screening criteria (Wilson-Jungner): important health problem, understood natural history, recognizable early stage, effective treatment available, suitable test, acceptable to population, cost-effective. Mammography uses low-dose X-rays to detect breast cancer; digital breast tomosynthesis (3D mammography) improves cancer detection, reduces false positives. Colonoscopy visualizes entire colon, allows polyp removal; alternatives include FIT (fecal immunochemical test), stool DNA (Cologuard), CT colonography. PSA (prostate-specific antigen) blood test for prostate cancer screening is controversial due to overdiagnosis of indolent cancers. Low-dose CT for lung cancer screening in high-risk smokers. Cervical cancer screening: Pap smear (cytology) and HPV testing. Screening intervals and ages vary by guideline organization (USPSTF, ACS, specialty societies).
